title: Myrsine oliveri
intro: Myrsine oliveri, also known as the New Zealand matagouri, is a woody shrub native to the South Island of New Zealand. The plant has been used in traditional Maori medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are said to aid in wound healing and reduce fever. The bioactive constituents of Myrsine oliveri include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from Myrsine oliveri include teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to treat colds, coughs, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Myrsine oliveri is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Myrsinaceae. It belongs to the genus Myrsine and is native to New Zealand. The plant is also known as the New Zealand matipo. Myrsine oliveri is a shrub or small tree that is commonly found in the wild throughout New Zealand. Its classification is further supported by its distinctive characteristics and growth habits.
uses: Myrsine oliveri is known for its medicinal properties in traditional Maori medicine, where it is used to treat various ailments. The plant's leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making them useful for wound healing and skin conditions. Myrsine oliveri is also used to treat digestive issues and as an expectorant to relieve respiratory problems. Its roots and stems are utilized in the treatment of fever and rheumatism. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Myrsine oliveri's medicinal uses.

The plant is also reported to have antioxidant and antifungal properties, making it useful for wound healing and skin protection.
